highlights.summary: Before a single pitch was thrown on Wednesday night at Marrs Park, the Allnighters were presented with an omen. A deceased raccoon was discovered on the field - stone cold, belly up, and in no condition to move itself. (P) Parello, ever the problem solver, decided to relocate the carcass approximately five feet to the left, under the apparent theory that five feet of distance would neutralize the bouquet of decomposing wildlife wafting across the infield. It did not. But none of that mattered once the game started because what followed was one of the most complete and dominant performances the Allnighters have put together all season in a 15-3 demolition of the Goonies. Where do you even start. Brendan (BTro) Trohon was back in the lineup after his tree-planting sabbatical and immediately went 3-for-5 with 4 RBI reminding everyone exactly what this team looks like when its full roster shows up. Pat (Hoz) Hosman went 3-for-4 with 4 RBI of his own at third base and held the hot corner down all night. Joe (Z) Zametjis contributed a clutch hit and RBI in his three at bats. Thomas (Dembo) Dembowski got things going from the leadoff spot going 3-for-5 with 2 RBI. Jason (Farrell) Farrell went 3-for-5 being Jason Farrell. Ryan (Ry) Overberg reached base every time he came up going 2-for-2 with 2 walks, an RBI, and 2 runs scored. Anthony (Ant) Gagliano went 3-for-3 with a walk and an RBI. Assad (DaaBoo) Daaboul went 2-for-4 with an RBI. Coach Baz went 1-for-4 with an RBI. Mike (The OG) Russo went 2-for-4 with an RBI AND delivered another gem on the mound holding the Goonies to just 3 runs in 6 innings. But the single greatest moment of the evening - and possibly the season - came when Joe Z was playing right field and a Goonie tried to stretch a hit into an extra base. Zametjis uncorked an absolute laser from right field to third base that traveled on a straight line with the precision of a surgical instrument, and Ryan Overberg did not move his glove even one inch as the ball arrived perfectly on target to gun down the runner. The dugout lost its collective mind. The Nighters needed this one badly and they delivered in every phase of the game. Two more wins next week and this team is right back in the thick of the playoff race where they belong. Dead raccoon and all. Go Nighters!

If someone had told you before Game 11 that the Allnighters would go into Murph Field shorthanded against the first-place Paddy Kelleys - a team stacked with ringers, supplemented by several players who by all biological logic probably should not still be playing competitive softball, and accompanied by what appeared to be their entire extended family spread across the backstop like a well-organized beach picnic - and still keep it within four runs, you would have called that a moral victory. And honestly you would have been right. The Kelleys brought everyone. We are talking players, family members, small children running unsupervised near the field, fold-out chairs, what may have been a portable grill, and enough energy to fill Fenway Park. The Allnighters, meanwhile, were missing several starters and showed up running on fumes and competitive pride. Things started with a genuinely promising sign when BMac had the audacity to challenge the umpire on a pitch ruling - the classic moon ball debate - and somehow the ump sided WITH him, which in the annals of Allnighters umpire relations is roughly equivalent to a solar eclipse. Hope flickered. And then Joe (Z) Zametjis, doing exactly the right thing as a baseball player, twice attempted to throw out the Paddy Kelleys pitcher at first base, and twice his Tommy John arm had other ideas, sailing the ball wide and directly into the wall of PK fans and family members assembled behind the bench. This triggered a complete meltdown from the Kelleys faithful, who seemed to believe that a player making a throw to first base in a baseball game was some kind of war crime, particularly because one overthrow came uncomfortably close to a toddler who had been left to roam the infield vicinity completely unsupervised. Coach Baz stood up immediately for Joe and the team - correctly pointing out that this is a ball field, and that throwing to first base is quite literally the point - but the Kelleys faithful were having none of it, and later decided that sliding into second base with the force of a linebacker twice in the same game was an appropriate response. In the middle of all this chaos the Allnighters somehow managed to play baseball. Russo pitched his heart out keeping it as close as possible, Thomas (Dembo) Dembowski went 3-for-4, Bob (Baz) Barrows went a spotless 3-for-3 with a run, and Eric (E) Moody and Jason (Farrell) Farrell each drove in a run. But the short lineup and the carnival atmosphere proved to be too much to overcome and the Kelleys held on for the win. Game 3 between these two teams later in the season just became the most anticipated matchup in Division 3. Go Nighters!

The Allnighters came into Monday night's matchup against the Wolfpack riding the bitter aftertaste of a loss to Franchise and desperately needing what should have been a relaxing cruise-control win. They got one out of two. The win? Absolutely. The relaxing part? Not even close. Mike (The OG) Russo took the mound and was once again absolutely untouchable, dealing a gem that kept the Wolfpack completely off balance all night while the Allnighters defense showed up looking like the championship team everyone knows they are. The offense had its sparks too - BMac went a perfect 4-for-4 with 3 RBI, Joe Z and Farrell both went 3-for-something with multiple RBI, Chris (P) Parello quietly went 3-for-3 with a pair of doubles and an RBI, and even Coach Baz went 4-for-4 proving that old age and experience still beats youth and enthusiasm in slow pitch softball. But the real story of the night had nothing to do with baseball. The Wolfpack brought in a fill-in player who apparently misread the vibe of a recreational Division 3 softball league and decided this was the time and place to audition for a reality TV show about unhinged behavior. After a mild disagreement with Eric (E) Moody, this gentleman decided to escalate - first trying to start a fight with Eric, then pivoting to Coach Baz, then expanding his grievances to include the entire Allnighters roster, then the fans in the stands, and finally the umpire himself. The umpire, to his enormous credit, ejected the man from the game, the next game, the entire season, and - rumor has it - was considering a restraining order from the greater Peabody area entirely. In the grand tradition of C League Little Boys softball, what should have been a breezy Monday night ended up being genuinely memorable. The Nighties got the W they needed and got a story they will be telling for years. Go Nighters!

For a game that on paper should have had the electricity of two championship finalists meeting in a rematch for the ages, Wednesday night against Franchise had less energy than someone writing out their tax check in the porto potty in centerfield at Marrs. And as such, the Allnighters dropped a sleepy 8-3 decision in a game that was about as exciting as staring at the blue liquid and everything on top of it in that same porto potty. There really is not much to say about a slow pitch softball team that scores 3 runs and collects fewer than 10 hits, so we will keep this appropriately brief and get everyone back to their lives. Several starters were absent and the bench, despite being deep on paper, just never woke up. On D, Ground balls disappeared into the dust, behind the dish an umpire who appeared to have last called games at a t-ball clinic was behind the plate, and the whole affair had the vibe of a team that collectively forgot to not only set Eric's alarm but their own. The game started with genuine promise when Pat (Hoz) Hosman crushed a solo home run on the very first at bat, sending a momentary jolt of electricity through the dugout - until someone quietly pointed out that if your biggest cheer of the night is for a solo home run in slow pitch softball, the evening is not going well. The balloon fizzed fast. Some genuine bright spots did exist though. Assad (DaaBoo) Daaboul - the Ninja - pulled off one of his signature Twilight Zone at bats, whiffing at one pitch to lull the defense to sleep before absolutely dunking a double over their heads on the next one, leaving the fielders confused and the bench absolutely losing their minds because nobody can quite explain how Assad operates and honestly it is better that way. Brendan (BTro) Trohon hit a ball so high it briefly achieved low Earth orbit before the shortstop casually settled under it and squeezed it for the out, because of course he did. Mike (The OG) Russo was the story on the mound, holding Franchise to just one earned run through five innings of absolutely lights-out pitching. When your ace gives you a performance like that and you still lose by five, the math is doing something deeply unflattering to your offense. And in the now-trademarked Allnighters fashion, the team had runners on base late with a chance to tie and take the lead, and stranded them with the efficiency of a team that has made leaving runners on base an actual art form. There is genuinely nothing more to report from this particular evening. The best advice is to flip the channel, find some Brady Bunch reruns, and rest up for Monday because the second half of this season starts now and this team is way too good to be sitting at 4-5. The bats will wake up. They always do. Go Nighters!

FINALLY. The Allnighters showed up with their bats, their gloves, and apparently a collective desire to remind everyone in Division 3 that yes, this is in fact the reigning championship team. The 20-8 demolition of the Pocket 9s on Monday night at Marrs Park was the kind of game that makes the season stats page look like it belongs to a legitimate 'ball organization rather than a group of boys who argue about RBIs in softball (and do they even track sac flys in softball??) and typically decide to take the night off 30 minutes before the game. It did take a few innings to get the engine fully running because apparently even a muscle car needs to warm up, but once the Nighters got rolling the Pocket 9s had about as much chance as a paper umbrella in a hurricane. Pat (Hoz) Hosman was the offensive headliner going a perfect 4-for-4 with an opposite field solo home run in the third that had everyone nodding approvingly - though let us be honest, solo home runs in slow pitch are a bit like ordering a salad at a steakhouse. You appreciate the effort but we all know what we came here for. Hoz is the real deal though and this leadoff performance was vintage. Brendan (BMac) McCarthy and Coach Baz both went 4-for-4 in what can only be described as a veteran masterclass in offensive leadership, proving that old does not mean slow - it just means experienced. Darren (Dermbo) McDermott was once again a machine at shortstop going 3-for-4 and making the position look like his personal property. Eric (E) Moody had another strong night both in the field and at the plate because apparently he decided to just be excellent at everything this season. Assad (DaaBoo) Daaboul ripped a gorgeous double and used his speed to leg it out which sent the bench into an absolute frenzy because nobody had seen Assad run that fast since the last time someone offered free food after the game. Joe (Z) Zametjis was the clutch guy again delivering hits when rallies needed fuel. Ryan (Ry) Overberg showed a little rookie patience at the plate but his walk approach keeps him dangerous at the top of the order and the results will come. Mike (The OG) Russo was another gem on the mound AND contributed some clutch hits at the plate because apparently pitching seven innings is not enough of a contribution for this man. As for (P) Parello, his batting average is starting to look a lot like his waistline these days - dwindling. Maybe he needs to rethink this whole diet and put on a few pounds for the second half of the season because the bats need every ounce of power they can find. Jason (Farrell) Farrell appears to still be stuck somewhere in pollen season - get that man an inhaler and possibly a lawyer. And Thomas (Dembo) Dembowski is starting to look like the Thomas of old which is a genuinely exciting development for the top of the lineup. The crowning achievement of the night was reaching the 12-run mercy rule for the first time this season - a milestone that was celebrated accordingly. When this team gels they are genuinely unstoppable. Thank goodness there are 21 games in a season because that gel historically does not seem to take hold until late July. But tonight it took hold, and it was glorious. Go Nighters!

The Allnighters dropped a tough 11-8 decision to the As on Wednesday night at Marrs Park in a game that felt like it was written by the auther of Batman and someone who really had it out for the Nighters. For the third time this season, Brendan (BTro) Trohon was absent from the lineup - but this time it was not about love, it was about trees. Yes, trees. BTro apparently chose to plant foliage for his day job over playing softball, which means the Allnighters are now officially losing games to a man's passion for landscaping. The gap he left in the lineup and the outfield turned out to be exactly the gap in the final score. Making matters worse before the first pitch, Coach Baz showed up without the scorebook AGAIN, forcing him to cobble together the lineup and box score on the back of what witnesses described as a gas station receipt.  Only to have to use an actual Batman book as his writing pad, oh the relevance on this night. Some things never change. On the mound, Russo was a little more challenged than usual tonight, issuing 5 walks and losing some of his command, but he buckled down and held the As to just 2 hits and 3 runs through the first four innings, keeping the Nighters very much in the game. The real story of the night though was a tale of two offenses. The Allnighters kept hitting the ball directly into the gloves of As defenders, making their fielders look like they were taking ground balls during pregame warmups, while the As were hitting rockets that had Nighters defenders diving, sprawling, and performing acts of athleticism that nobody planned for. And even with all that, the Nighters defense actually had the better night overall - with one glaring exception. Their right center fielder showed up channeling his inner Batman, striking the Nighties with two absolutely ridiculous catches that flat out stole the game. He robbed the Nighters in the sixth inning to kill a rally, came back in the seventh to do it again, and collectively the Allnighters left 10 runners stranded on base which is becoming the unofficial team slogan of 2026. The bright spots were real though - Darren (Dermbo) McDermott was an absolute machine going a perfect 3-for-3 with 3 singles doing exactly what a shortstop should do every single night, while Ryan (Ry) Overberg matched him going 3-for-3 as well with an RBI and a run. Eric (E) Moody came up huge in the middle of the order going 2-for-3 with a triple and 2 RBI, while Ant had a powerful shot down the left field line that got him a double, and Joe (Z) Zametjis continued his torrid season going 2-for-2 with a double, an RBI, 2 runs, and a walk to keep the offense moving. And in perhaps the most celebrated moment of the night, Assad (DaaBoo) Daaboul singled for his first hit of the season, sending the dugout into a frenzy that was completely disproportionate to the game situation but one hundred percent deserved. Coach Baz, choosing strategy over sentimentality, dropped the bottom two spots in the order in the seventh to get the top of the lineup one last crack at the game - but Spider-Man made his curtain call appearance in right center and robbed (P) Parello to end it. No worries. This team has proven it can play with anyone in this division, and there are still plenty of games left in the regular season. The Nighters will be standing at the top where they belong when it counts. Go Nighters!

If the Bermuda Triangle and Fantasy Island and Bar Crawl Comedy had a baby and named it softball, it would look exactly like what happened on Monday night at Murphy Park when the Allnighters dropped a heartbreaking 16-12 nine-inning thriller to the Goonies. The madness began before the sun even came up, when Coach Baz was jolted awake at 5:30am, and no it was Tattoo yelling Da Plane Da Plane, it was by Brendan (BTro) Trohon yelling Da Game Da Game, ok he was texting and was asking if the game was on - but it felt like Tattoo since Baz was in full dream with his future love of his life, only to of course BTro somehow still arrived late. From there the night had more twists and turns than a roller coaster at Canobie Lake Park or Tazers at a Bar Crawl stand up comedy night. The team showed up in good spirits with the sun doing its best to warm up a cold June day, but in true Allnighters fashion, several starters strolled in after game time while the crowd waited impatiently for the first pitch. Trohon, looking like an inmate cleaning the highway, pulled on his uniform and cleats while the batter was already in the box - only to then discover his brother had brought his uniform but somehow forgot his glove. Who does that? Who grabs the jersey but leaves the glove? Enough of the Moby Dick saga about Trohon. The Goonies wasted no time showing their power in the first inning, hammering some rock hard shots and putting up 4 before the Nighters could blink. Darren (Dermbo) McDermott realized quickly these were not the fat and slow Holden boys when one of their hitters beat out a scorched ground ball that would have been an easy out any other night. But Russo and the defense tightened up, got out of the inning, and just like that the Nighters caught fire and answered with 5 runs of their own in the bottom of the first, with BTro hitting which could be the furthest ball hit at Radden, to let the Goonies know exactly who they were dealing with. The defending champs looked alive and dangerous. The next couple of innings both teams settled into a rhythm, and the Nighters kept the pressure on to put up another 3 in the third, grabbing a comfortable 9-6 lead heading into the fourth. And that is when Fantasy Island made its turn toward Horror Island. The OBI curse - One Bad Inning - the infamous Allnighters affliction that no amount of Gatorade or therapy has been able to cure, struck without warning. After a couple of quiet offensive innings while Russo and the defense held firm, BTro crushed an absolute bomb that should have opened the floodgates, but Jason (Farrell) Farrell, unable to decide whether to tag or advance to second, got caught in no man's land and was nearly rear-ended by the charging Trohon at second base, forcing him to scramble to third only to get thrown out. That baserunning blunder sucked all the life out of the dugout, and the Goonies smelled blood. In the top of the sixth, the visitors erupted for 4 runs on what felt like 99 Allnighters errors, and just like that Tattoo appeared on the Fantasy Island dock shooting daggers at the bench. But just when everyone thought the plane was coming to take the Nighters home with an L, the champs in this dugout refused to go quietly. Coach Baz stepped in to pinch hit for Russo and delivered a spark, the top of the lineup came alive, and before anyone knew what was happening the game was tied and the Allnighters had a chance to win it in regulation. Joe (Z) Zametjis gave everything he had trying to thread a liner into the outfield gap but the Goonies second baseman had it played perfectly, and just like that we were in extra innings. (P) Parello took the mound for the eighth and ninth and immediately confused the Goonies with his vintage slider approach - one kid looked absolutely lost and struck out on two pitches sending the Nighters dugout into a frenzy. It felt like Fantasy Island was finally delivering on its promise. But what followed was a mix of jawing, smack talk, and ultimately the Nighters fading in the bottom of the eighth at the plate and then heading to the outfield in the ninth looking dazed and confused. The Goonies bats heated up for the first time since the first inning, every batted ball seemed to find a Nighter glove only to bounce out, and they pushed across 4 in the ninth to go up 16-12. Even with Thomas (Dembo) Dembowski, intentional confusion here to not let the Goons know who is who since Dembo was on the bench and we definitely want Thomas leading us in the ring especially with his (your five five comment), letting their pitcher know he was absolutely not intimidated at the plate, the deficit was too much and the Allnighters fell 16-12 in nine hard-fought innings. Dermbo McDermott deserves a special mention finishing the night with roughly 10 putouts at short, absolutely vacuuming up everything in his zip code and single-handedly keeping the Nighters in the game defensively for most of the night, while Ryan the rook showed great promise with his patience at the bat and handling first. The positives - this team showed they can hang with the best in the division offensively and defensively and have the heart of champions. The negative - the offense and defense both caught the last boat off Fantasy Island at the same time and could not hold the lead when it mattered most. Up next on Wednesday it is the A's. Will this be a Fantasy Island horror sequel, or will Tattoo finally bring the babes and cocktails after the game? Stay tuned. Go Nighters!

The Allnighters gutted out a wild 11-10 win over Holden Station on Tuesday night at Marrs Park in a game that had more plot twists than the season finale of Stranger Things. For the second straight game, (Loverboy) BTro was nowhere to be found, leaving the lineup without its big gun yet again. Before the first pitch was even thrown, the pre-game chaos began when the umpires from the prior game had no idea another game was scheduled, leaving the Nighters briefly convinced they had no umps. But in a twist of divine comedy, because the team had complained about one of the umps the week prior, the league sent in a brand new crew just for the Allnighters - so the boys cheerfully waved goodbye to the old ump as he walked off. The new crew however brought their own brand of Twilight Zone energy, incorrectly calling three outfield plays as catches in what could only be described as a collective hallucination, and Tommy (Dembo) Dembowski was flat out robbed of what would have been an ESPN SportsCenter catch of the year. Adding to the pre-game circus, Eric (E) Moody overslept at 7pm somehow convinced the game started at 9, forcing Coach Baz to suit up at third base. Baz held his own out there making some solid plays, but the nearly 40-year-old glove he was forced to use - borrowed from (P) Parello, because of course it was - literally disintegrated on a hard liner, sending the ball rolling free and gifting Holden Station two unearned runs that Mike (The OG) Russo certainly did not deserve. Russo was nothing short of stellar on the mound all night, pitching a gutsy complete game and single-handedly keeping the Allnighters in it despite a parade of errors and unearned runs behind him. Joe Z was the offensive engine going a perfect 4-for-4 with a double and three RBI, while Pat (Hoz) Hosman turned what looked like a routine fly ball into a two-run blast over the fence that had everyone doing a double take. Darren (Dermbo) McDermott was back in classic form going 3-for-4 with three RBI looking every bit the stellar shortstop the team needs him to be. Dembo Dembowski got his bat going again from the leadoff spot driving in two and helping the top of the lineup click. Jason (Farrell) Farrell looked like his old self again crossing the plate three times, and Tyler (Thomas) Evans pulled out a nice double to keep things moving. (P) Parello got his back going, well slightly, with one hit. Assad (DaaBoo) Daaboul made some great contact showing flashes of the hitter everyone knows he can be, though he did strike out again because of course he did. Eric finally woke up and made the trek to the field, showed a nice glove at third but needed a little warm-up time to get his throws down and stop playing stare-down with baserunners. The key play of the entire game came in the final inning when Anthony (Ant) Gagliano made a heads-up fielding play at second and gunned down the lead runner at the plate - and then their cleanup hitter flew out to end the threat. The cavalry arrived late when Brendan (BMac) McCarthy came rushing straight from his kid's game to pinch hit and delivered exactly when the team needed it most, drawing a walk and ripping a hit to help spark the late rally that sealed the deal. A gutsy, scrappy, chaotic, only-the-Allnighters kind of win. Go Nighters!

The Allnighters dropped a tough one to the Paddy Kelleys 11-5 on Wednesday night at Marrs Park, but honestly the scoreboard was the least bizarre thing about the evening. Before the first pitch was even thrown, Coach Baz forgot the scorebook, Dermbo McDermott was spotted trying to grab a taco while the entire team stood on the diamond staring at the parking lot waiting for him to show up, and Farrell had to leave early after his allergies escalated to what witnesses described as full cardiac arrest. Then Mike (The Stoic) Stoico somehow managed to break his finger on what should have been a routine fly ball - which he dropped of course. Rod Serling himself could not have written a stranger opening act. With all that pre-game chaos it is a miracle the Nighters even laced up their cleats let alone scored 5. The Allnighters were also missing their big gun Brendan (BTro) Trohon who chose love over ball this day and would have provided some much needed pop in a lineup that desperately needed it. As if the night needed any more Twilight Zone energy, the infamous OBI curse - One Bad Inning - made its grand return, as the Nighters gifted the Kelleys a crooked number through a series of errors that would make a little league coach weep. And to add true insult to injury, after the other team launched a bomb homerun, Thomas thought it would be a great time to fire Russo a fresh ball - drilling him square in the back of the head. Nothing says team chemistry like a concussion from your own guy. Meanwhile the Kelleys showed up with a ringer who apparently moonlights as a human trebuchet, launching not one but TWO bombs into the neighboring park. And if that was not enough, this same hired gun had the audacity to reach over the fence and rob Pat Hosman of what would have been a legitimate homerun - plucking it right off the top of the wall. On the bright side Anthony (Ant) Gagliano and Mike (The OG) Russo both went 2-for-2 at the plate, making everyone question why these two are not hitting at the top of the order - especially when two other Nighters struck out, which as any slow pitch player knows, should be physically impossible. Coach himself made a highlight reel catch on a fly ball that required him to run what felt like a full mile, which for a man whose cardio routine consists of walking to the fridge is frankly heroic. And through it all Russo was the only reason the score was not uglier, holding the Kelleys to 5 earned runs over 7 innings despite the circus unfolding behind him. The Kelleys may have doubled their payroll and brought in ringers but the Nighters are not sweating it. Everyone needs to hit the cages this week (or at minimum the Cab) and the Allnighters will be back in first place where they belong. Go Nighters!

Allnighters walked past the Wolfpack 11-4 at Marrs Park on Monday in a dominant home performance. The Nighters jumped out early with two runs in the first inning aided by excessive Wolfpack walks, and never looked back. Joe Z led the offense going 3-for-4 with a double and driving in two, while Eric (E) Moody went 2-for-2. Darren (Dermbo) McDermott, Jason Farrell, Coach Baz, and Mike Russo each chipped in with key hits. A positive sign on the night was very few runners left on base and most outs were hard-hit balls. Mike (The OG) Russo was dominant on the mound again, going all 7 innings allowing just 4 runs on 10 hits. Even a long bomb by the Wolfpack in the 7th did not rattle Russo or the Nighters. The defense, especially the golden outfield crew, made some clutch catches in the middle innings to truly deliver a heart punch to the Pack. Next up - the newly stacked Paddy Kelleys who appear to have doubled their payroll in one last push for glory. The Nighters will need to bring the juice!

Allnighters could not hold onto the lead against Pocket 9s on Wednesday at Raddin Park and fell 5-4 despite leading by three going into the bottom of the 5th. Robert (The Godfather) Barrows was the offensive highlight for AllNighters going 3 for 3 with three line shots to the right side, while Patrick Hosman, Brendan (Btro) Trohon, and Ryan Overberg each added two hits. A single by Overberg put AllNighters on the board in the top of the second and then the crew rallied in the 4th. AllNighters collected 14 hits in the game. Unfortunately they left 11 men on base, hit into two double plays, and stranded the bases loaded in the top of the 7th. Barrows, Overberg, and Joe Zametjis each drove in one run. Pocket 9s finished things off with a walk-off single RBI in the bottom of the 7th. If the Allnighters look to repeat this year, they better get their bats a little warmer.

Allnighters defeated Franchise 9-5 on Monday. The Nighters jumped out to the lead in the bottom of the first inning after Brendan (Btro) Trohon singled scoring one run, Joe Zametjis homered scoring three runs, and Darren (Dermbo) McDermott singled scoring one run. A single by Brendan (Bmac) McCarthy, a single by Trohon, and a double by Jason Farrell helped AllNighters extend their early lead in the second. Mike (The OG) Russo earned the win for AllNighters. The starting pitcher gave up seven hits and five runs over seven innings striking out none and walking none. AllNighters collected 14 hits in the game. Patrick Hosman, McCarthy, Eric (E) Moody, and Trohon each collected two hits. Zametjis led AllNighters with three runs batted in going 1-for-3 on the day.
